body_text:
Section 5.4 Products of Rational and Trigonometric Functions

EXAMPLE 2 Applying Jordan's lemma
Derive the identity
$$
\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \frac{x \sin x}{x^{2}+a^{2}} d x=\frac{\pi}{e^{a}}, \quad a \text { any real number }>0
$$

Solution Consider the contour integral over the closed semi-circle $\gamma_{R}$
$$
I_{\gamma_{R}}=\int_{\sigma_{R}} \frac{z}{z^{2}+a^{2}} e^{i z} d z+\int_{-R}^{R} \frac{x}{x^{2}+a^{2}} e^{i x} d x=I_{\sigma_{R}}+I_{R}
$$
(Figure 5). By Jordan's lemma (Corollary 1), $\lim _{R \rightarrow \infty} I_{\sigma_{R}}=0$. For $R>$

d poles
1 in Ex-
$$
I_{\gamma_{R}}=2 \pi i \operatorname{Res}\left(\frac{z e^{i z}}{z^{2}+a^{2}}, i a\right)=2 \pi i \frac{(i a) e^{i(i a)}}{2(i a)}=\frac{\pi}{e^{a}} i
$$

Taking the limit as $R \rightarrow \infty$ in (11) and using the fact that $I_{\sigma_{R}} \rightarrow 0$, we get
$$
\frac{\pi}{e^{a}} i=\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \frac{x}{x^{2}+a^{2}} e^{i x} d x=\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \frac{x \cos x}{x^{2}+a^{2}} d x+i \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \frac{x \sin x}{x^{2}+a^{2}} d x
$$
and the desired identity follows upon taking imaginary parts on both sides.
Indenting Contours
If we let $a \rightarrow 0$ in Example 2 and do not worry about justifying taki the limit inside the integral, we get the formula
$$
\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \frac{\sin x}{x} d x=\pi
$$

This answer is indeed correct and yields an important integral that aris in many applications. In the remainder of this section, we will develop method to calculate this and other interesting integrals.

We need to return to the idea of Cauchy principal values of integrals. Section 5.3 we defined the Cauchy principal value of an improper integ over the real line. However, an integral can also be improper if the integra becomes unbounded at a point inside the interval of integration. To ma our discussion concrete, consider
$$
\int_{-1}^{1} f(x) d x
$$
where $f(x)$ is continuous on $[-1,0)$ and $(0,1]$ but might have infinite limi as $x$ approaches 0 from the left or right. Such an integral is said to be cor vergent if both $\lim _{b \rightarrow 0^{-}} \int_{-1}^{b} f(x) d x$ and $\lim _{a \rightarrow 0^{+}} \int_{a}^{1} f(x) d x$ are convergen In this case, we set (Figure 6)
